name: MeepleAI Custom Board Game QA
description: Custom evaluation dataset for MeepleAI RAG pipeline. 35 Q&A pairs across 3 complexity levels covering Catan (simple), Terraforming Mars (medium), and Spirit Island (complex).

samples.question: What are the five resource types in Catan?
samples.expected_answer: The five resource types are brick, lumber, wool, grain, and ore.

samples.question: How many resource cards does each terrain hex produce when its number is rolled?
samples.expected_answer: Each terrain hex produces 1 resource card for every settlement adjacent to it, and 2 resource cards for every city adjacent to it.

samples.question: What resources are needed to build a city in Catan?
samples.expected_answer: 3 ore and 2 grain are needed to upgrade a settlement to a city.

samples.question: What is the placement rule for settlements in Catan?
samples.expected_answer: Settlements must be placed on an unoccupied intersection that is at least two intersections away from any other settlement or city (the distance rule).

samples.question: How do ports work in Catan?
samples.expected_answer: Ports allow better trade ratios with the bank. A general port allows 3:1 trades, while specific resource ports allow 2:1 trades for that resource. You must build a settlement on a port to use it.

samples.question: What is the Knight card in Catan and how is Largest Army determined?
samples.expected_answer: The Knight card lets you move the robber to a new hex and steal a resource from an adjacent player. The player who plays the most Knight cards (minimum 3) receives the Largest Army card worth 2 victory points.

samples.question: Can you play a development card on the same turn you buy it in Catan?
samples.expected_answer: No, you cannot play a development card on the same turn you bought it. You must wait until a subsequent turn to play it.

samples.question: What happens during initial placement in Catan?
samples.expected_answer: Players take turns placing 1 settlement and 1 road in order, then in reverse order place a second settlement and road. The second settlement produces starting resources.

samples.question: What are the six standard project card types in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: The six types are: Event (red, one-time), Automated (green, permanent), Active (blue, ongoing actions), Earth (brown, corporations), Space (gray, space cards), and City (orange, city placement).

samples.question: What are the three global parameters in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: The three global parameters are temperature, oxygen level, and ocean coverage. The game ends when all three reach their target values.

samples.question: How does the Terraforming Rating work in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: Terraforming Rating (TR) starts at 20 and represents both your score and your income. Each time you raise a global parameter, your TR increases by 1. TR also determines your MegaCredit income each generation.

samples.question: What are the six production types in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: The six production types are MegaCredits, Steel, Titanium, Plants, Energy, and Heat. Production happens during the production phase of each generation.

samples.question: How does steel reduce the cost of building cards in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: Each steel resource reduces the cost of cards with a building tag by 2 MegaCredits. Steel can only be used for building-tagged cards.

samples.question: What happens during the production phase in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: During production: (1) energy is converted to heat, (2) players receive MegaCredits equal to their TR plus MegaCredit production, and (3) all other resources are produced according to each player's production track values.

samples.question: How do standard projects work in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: Standard projects are actions available to all players without needing cards. They include selling patents (cards for 1 MC each), power plant (11 MC for +1 energy production), asteroid (14 MC for +1 temperature), aquifer (18 MC for 1 ocean), greenery (23 MC for 1 greenery), and city (25 MC for 1 city).

samples.question: How is final scoring calculated in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: Final score equals TR plus VP from cards, plus VP from awards (5/2/0), plus VP from milestones (5), plus 1 VP per greenery tile, plus VP from city tiles (1 VP per adjacent greenery).

samples.question: What are milestones in Terraforming Mars and how many can be claimed?
samples.expected_answer: Milestones are achievements that can be claimed by paying 8 MC when you meet the requirement. Only 3 milestones can be claimed per game, each worth 5 VP.

samples.question: How many cards do players draft at the start of each generation in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: Players are dealt 4 cards at the start of each generation and may buy any number of them for 3 MC each. Unpurchased cards are discarded.

samples.question: How many spirit panels does each player get in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Each player gets 1 spirit panel showing their unique spirit with starting innate powers, growth options, presence track, and special rules.

samples.question: What are the phases of a turn in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Each turn has the following phases: (1) Spirit Phase (growth, gain energy, play cards), (2) Fast Power Phase, (3) Invader Phase (explore, build, ravage), (4) Slow Power Phase, (5) Time Passes (discard, reset).

samples.question: How does the Invader Explore phase work in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: During Explore, flip the top invader card. Invader explorers are added to each land of the shown terrain type that is adjacent to a town, city, or ocean.

samples.question: What happens when invaders ravage in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Invaders in the ravaged land deal damage equal to their total strength (explorers=1, towns=2, cities=3). If total damage is 2+, the land takes 1 blight. Dahan in the land counterattack with 2 damage each.

samples.question: What is Fear and how does it help in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Fear is generated by spirits destroying invaders or using powers. When enough fear is generated to fill the fear pool, a Fear Card is earned. Fear cards provide beneficial effects at the start of the invader phase and change victory conditions.

samples.question: How does blight work in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: When a land takes 2+ damage from invaders, 1 blight is added. If a land already has blight, adding another cascades 1 blight to an adjacent land. The blight card flips when the island's blight pool is empty, often adding a penalty.

samples.question: What are the win conditions in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Players win by achieving the current Terror Level victory condition. Terror Level 1: no invaders remain on the island. Terror Level 2: no towns or cities remain. Terror Level 3: no cities remain. Terror Level 4 (from fear card): instant victory.

samples.question: How does presence placement work in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: During growth, spirits place presence from their spirit panel onto the island. Removing presence reveals bonuses on the tracks (card plays, energy per turn). Presence cannot be placed in ocean or lands without existing presence unless specified.

samples.question: What is the difference between fast and slow powers in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Fast powers resolve before the invader phase, allowing preemptive action. Slow powers resolve after the invader phase. The timing difference is critical for strategy as fast powers can prevent damage while slow powers react to it.

samples.question: How does energy work in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Spirits gain energy each turn based on their presence track. Energy is spent to play power cards and is not limited. Unspent energy carries over between turns.

samples.question: What is the difference between Minor and Major powers in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Minor powers cost 0-1 energy and have modest effects. Major powers cost 2+ energy, have powerful effects, but require forgetting (permanently discarding) a power card when gained. Both are gained through growth actions.

samples.question: How are oceans placed in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: Oceans are placed on reserved ocean spaces on the board. When placed, they increase oxygen by 0 and are not owned by any player. Each ocean tile placed gives 2 MC bonus to the placer for each adjacent ocean tile.

samples.question: What is the Invader Board in Spirit Island and how does it function?
samples.expected_answer: The Invader Board has three slots: Explore, Build, and Ravage. Invader cards advance right each turn. The newest card triggers Explore, the middle triggers Build, and the oldest triggers Ravage, creating a predictable escalation pattern.

samples.question: What are the loss conditions in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Players lose if any spirit has no presence on the island, or if the blight card instructions say so (usually when the blight pool is exhausted and the flipped card's condition triggers), or if the invader deck runs out.

samples.question: How many actions can a player take per turn in Terraforming Mars?
samples.expected_answer: During the action phase, players take turns performing 1 or 2 actions per turn, or pass. Actions include playing a card, using a standard project, claiming a milestone, funding an award, converting resources, or using a card action.

samples.question: What determines the number of invader cards placed during setup in Spirit Island based on player count?
samples.expected_answer: In a 1-player game, use 1 board and the stage II invader cards are reduced. In 2-player, use 2 boards. The number of starting blight equals 2 per player plus 1. The fear pool size is 4 fear tokens per player.

samples.question: How do innate powers work in Spirit Island?
samples.expected_answer: Innate powers are printed on the spirit panel and activate automatically when you play cards with matching elements. They have threshold levels - if you play enough cards with the required elements, higher thresholds activate for stronger effects.
